behaviors.description: Only files changed in the pull request are analyzed — unchanged files are not scanned.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires real Git repository access to determine which files actually changed in the PR. A unit test with mocked Git output could verify the code calls the Git API, but couldn't catch the failure mode where the code ignores the change list and scans the entire repository instead. The real failure boundary is the filesystem: we need an actual Git repository with real files to verify the diff filtering actually works and doesn't fall back to scanning everything.
behaviors.test_description: Clone a Git repository with multiple files. Create a pull request (or simulate one with Git branches) that modifies exactly 5 files out of 20 total files. Run the linter against the PR context and verify via file access logs or instrumentation that only the 5 changed files are analyzed, never the other 15 unchanged files.

behaviors.description: Findings are grouped by severity (error, warning, info) in the posted PR comment.
behaviors.justification: Grouping findings by severity is pure data transformation logic with no I/O or external dependencies. We can unit test the grouping function with mocked findings of mixed severities and verify the output structure (three sections, correct order, complete coverage) without any GitHub API or file system access. The failure mode (wrong grouping order, missing severity, misclassification) is caught by unit tests with carefully crafted inputs.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test the grouping function with a mocked list of findings (5 errors, 3 warnings, 2 info messages, in random order). Verify the output contains three sections in order (errors, warnings, info), each section contains only findings of that severity, and all findings appear exactly once.

behaviors.description: A pull request that touches 200 files completes analysis within the 10-minute Actions timeout.
behaviors.justification: Performance is inherently a System-level concern. Mocking file reads and the linter process would always be fast and would never reveal actual bottlenecks (I/O contention, subprocess overhead, GC pauses). We must measure real file I/O performance, real linting subprocess execution time, and realistic network conditions to verify the system meets the timeout. A mock-based test would always pass while production could timeout.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test environment with 200 actual files (varying sizes: small, medium, large) with realistic complexity. Run the complete linter pipeline (file discovery, file reading, linting subprocess, output collection, grouping, GitHub posting simulation) on all 200 files. Measure total wall-clock time and verify it completes in under 5 minutes (leaving margin against the 10-minute timeout).

behaviors.description: When the linter process crashes on a malformed file, the remaining files are still analyzed and results posted.
behaviors.justification: This behavior specifically tests process crash handling, not just exception handling. Real subprocess crashes (exit codes, signal termination, orphaned processes, partial stderr) behave differently from in-process exceptions. Integration tests with mock exceptions could pass while the real crash scenario fails (e.g., zombie process, incomplete output capture, signal handling incorrect). The real failure boundary is the subprocess layer: we need an actual linter process that crashes to verify the pipeline recovers and completes.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test with 10 files, including one malformed file that will cause the linter subprocess to crash (non-zero exit). Verify: (1) the crash is caught and does not propagate as unhandled exception, (2) the remaining 9 files are processed, (3) findings from non-crashed files are collected, and (4) results are posted (or logged) even though one file crashed. Confirm no zombie processes remain after the crash.

behaviors.description: Duplicate findings on the same line from overlapping rules are deduplicated in the output.
behaviors.justification: Deduplication is pure data transformation logic. We can thoroughly test it with unit tests using mocked findings data. The failure modes (missing dedup, over-dedup, wrong key matching) are all caught by unit tests with carefully designed test cases—no external infrastructure or file I/O needed. The logic is deterministic and fully testable in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test the deduplication function with a mocked list of findings including: (1) exact duplicates (same file, line, rule, severity, message), (2) near-duplicates (same file and line but different rule or severity), and (3) unique findings. Verify that exact duplicates are removed (keeping only one), near-duplicates are kept, and all findings are present in the final output.
